title: Chevron Wheatstone Outage Tests LNG Reliability And Investor Expectations - simplywall.st
content:
For investors watching Chevron, ticker NYSE:CVX, the Wheatstone outage adds a new layer of risk to a stock that has recently seen strong momentum. The share price sits at $211.15, with returns of 4.7% over the past week, 13.1% over the past month, and 35.4% year to date. Longer term, the stock shows returns of 32.9% over 1 year, 46.9% over 3 years, and 145.9% over 5 years. This LNG disruption matters because Wheatstone is a core Australian asset at a time when global gas markets are already tight. Readers may want to watch how quickly operations normalize, how customer contract obligations are handled, and whether any commentary from Chevron hints at shifts in capital allocation or risk management around critical LNG infrastructure. Stay updated on the most important news stories for Chevron by adding it to your watchlist or portfolio. Alternatively, explore our Community to discover new perspectives on Chevron. 2 things going right for Chevron that this headline doesn't cover. The Wheatstone outage hits Chevron at a time when liquefied natural gas has been a key part of its integrated story alongside oil exposure. With Gorgon and Wheatstone both affected by recent weather events, Chevron now has to manage operational interruptions on assets that were being referenced as important suppliers for Asian buyers while global LNG balances are already tight. That can increase short term price support for LNG, yet it also highlights how concentrated and weather exposed some of Chevron’s export routes are compared with peers such as Exxon Mobil and Shell. For you, the outage is a reminder that operational reliability and infrastructure resilience matter just as much as headline production targets or new deals in places like Venezuela and offshore Greece. Knowing what a company is worth starts with understanding its story.

From here, watch how quickly Wheatstone progresses from partial restart to full output, any quantified guidance Chevron offers on lost volumes or repair costs, and whether insurers or partners share part of the burden. Monitor commentary at industry events such as CERAWeek and the Connected Worker summit for clues on how Chevron wants to use digital monitoring and remote operations to cut outage risk across LNG and upstream projects. It may also be useful to track how regulators respond to higher LNG prices, including any progress on an Australian windfall tax that could influence future returns from these assets. title: Warren Buffett Was Right: These Oil Stocks Are the Safest Bet in an Iran-Rattled Market - The Globe and Mail
content: Warren Buffett made Chevron and Occidental Petroleum two of Berkshire's 10 largest holdings. Both oil giants can thrive at much lower oil prices. They're really cashing in this year now that crude prices have surged. Warren Buffett made a bold bet on a couple of oil stocks before he retired as Berkshire Hathaway's (NYSE: BRKA)(NYSE: BRKB) CEO earlier this year. His company bought nearly 27% of Occidental Petroleum's (NYSE: OXY) outstanding shares and built a 6.5% stake in oil giant Chevron(NYSE: CVX), making them Berkshire's sixth- and fourth-largest holdings, respectively. Those moves have paid off this year as crude prices skyrocketed due to the war with Iran. Here's a look at why these top oil stocks remain a safe bet in today's Iran-rattled market. Will AI create the world's first trillionaire? Our team just released a report on the one little-known company, called an "Indispensable Monopoly" providing the critical technology Nvidia and Intel both need. Continue » Occidental has spent the past several years building a larger-scale, more focused oil company, thanks in part to Berkshire's help. Buffett's company helped Occidental fund its $38 billion acquisition of Anadarko Petroleum in 2019 through a preferred stock investment, enabling it to beat out a rival offer from Chevron. Occidental also acquired CrownRock for $12 billion in 2024. While those deals saddled Occidental with a lot of debt, it has steadily whittled down its borrowings by generating free cash flow and selling assets, including selling OxyChem to Berkshire for $9.7 billion earlier this year. As a result, Occidental can thrive at lower oil prices. It was on track to generate $1.2 billion of additional free cash flow this year without any increase in oil prices. Meanwhile, with crude oil soaring this year, Occidental will produce an even bigger free cash flow gusher, giving it additional funds to repay debt, repurchase stock, and eventually redeem Berkshire's preferred stock investment. Chevron has also spent the past few years upgrading its portfolio. It sold off several lower-margin assets and has invested heavily in developing and acquiring higher-margin assets. The oil giant completed several major growth capital projects last year and closed its $55 billion acquisition of Hess. As a result, Chevron expected to generate $12.5 billion of incremental free cash flow this year at $70 oil. With oil prices now much higher, it can produce an even bigger free cash flow gusher. The oil company can use those funds to further fortify its elite balance sheet and repurchase shares toward the top end of its $10 billion to $20 billion target range. Chevron's strategy of investing in low-cost, high-margin assets will really pay off in the coming years. It can generate enough cash to fund its capital program and dividend at sub-$50 oil. As a result, it can produce substantial free cash flow at higher prices. For example, it can grow its free cash flow at a more than 10% annual rate if crude averages $70 a barrel through 2030. Occidental Petroleum and Chevron both expected to generate meaningfully more free cash flow this year without any increase in crude prices. They're now on track to produce even bigger gushers now that prices are much higher. Their ability to thrive at lower oil prices shows that Buffett was right that they were safe bets to add to Berkshire's portfolio. Before you buy stock in Occidental Petroleum, consider this: The Motley Fool Stock Advisor analyst team just identified what they believe are the 10 best stocks for investors to buy now… and Occidental Petroleum wasn’t one of them. The 10 stocks that made the cut could produce monster returns in the coming years. Consider when Netflix made this list on December 17, 2004... if you invested $1,000 at the time of our recommendation, you’d have $503,861!* Or when Nvidia made this list on April 15, 2005... if you invested $1,000 at the time of our recommendation, you’d have $1,026,987!* Now, it’s worth noting Stock Advisor’s total average return is 884% — a market-crushing outperformance compared to 179% for the S&P 500. title: Cyclone Outages And Tax Threat Test Chevron’s Australian LNG Story - Yahoo! Finance Canada
content: Tip: Try a valid symbol or a specific company name for relevant results Find your next quality investment with Simply Wall St's easy and powerful screener, trusted by over 7 million individual investors worldwide. Cyclone related outages have disrupted Chevron's Gorgon and Wheatstone LNG production in Western Australia during an ongoing global LNG supply crunch. The Australian government is considering a windfall profits tax as LNG prices rise, adding potential regulatory and fiscal pressure for NYSE:CVX and other producers. These events raise questions around Chevron's production volumes, contract obligations, and regional LNG market share in the near term. For you as an investor, this matters because Chevron is a major global LNG supplier, and Gorgon and Wheatstone are core assets within its Australian portfolio. With LNG already tight, any disruption at these projects can affect how reliably Chevron supplies key buyers in Asia and beyond, as well as how its position compares with other producers in the region. The possibility of a windfall profits tax in Australia also puts a spotlight on Chevron's tax and regulatory exposure in one of its important LNG hubs. Understanding how these operational interruptions and potential policy changes fit into the broader NYSE:CVX investment case can help you assess risk, capital allocation priorities, and the balance between opportunity and uncertainty in its LNG business. Stay updated on the most important news stories for Chevron by adding it to your watchlist or portfolio. Alternatively, explore our Community to discover new perspectives on Chevron. Is Chevron's balance sheet strong enough for future acquisitions? Dive into our detailed financial health analysis. The cyclone-related outages at Gorgon and Wheatstone are an operational shock, but for investors the more structural question sits with Australia’s possible windfall profits tax. Disrupted LNG volumes can affect Chevron’s near term sales, contract flexibility, and any use of spot-market cargoes while facilities are brought fully back online. A windfall tax would sit on top of existing royalties and corporate taxes in one of Chevron’s key LNG regions, which could narrow project-level returns and influence how future capital is split between Australia and other LNG hubs such as the United States, Qatar, or projects involving Exxon Mobil and Shell. If higher taxes apply when prices spike, cash flows may become more volatile after tax than before tax. This kind of policy risk also connects with broader ESG and regulatory pressures that analysts already flag for larger hydrocarbon producers. The focus on operational efficiency and infrastructure resilience in the narrative is directly tested by unplanned cyclone outages, which show how important reliability is for long term LNG cash generation. Potential windfall taxation and higher compliance costs in Australia cut against the narrative’s expectation of structurally improving margins from large upstream projects. The possibility of a new profit-based levy on LNG exports from Australia is not clearly reflected in the narrative’s discussion of regulatory and tax pressures. Knowing what a company is worth starts with understanding its story. Check out one of the top narratives in the Simply Wall St Community for Chevron to help decide what it's worth to you. ⚠️ Concentrated LNG exposure in Australia increases sensitivity to local policy shifts such as a windfall profits tax and tighter domestic market rules. ⚠️ Weather-related outages highlight physical risk to large, long life assets that underpin LNG contracts and can affect Chevron’s reliability versus peers like Woodside, Shell, and Exxon Mobil. 🎁 A global LNG supply crunch can support pricing for uncontracted volumes, which may partly offset the financial impact of temporary production disruptions. 🎁 The situation may encourage Chevron to continue investing in digital operations and infrastructure resilience, aligning with industry efforts to improve uptime and asset reliability. From here, watch how quickly Gorgon and Wheatstone return to normal operations, any disclosure on lost volumes or use of force majeure clauses, and whether Chevron signals changes to capital spending in Australian LNG. Pay close attention to how the proposed windfall profits tax is designed, especially any price thresholds, duration, and treatment of existing long term contracts, as these details will shape longer term project economics. It is also useful to compare Chevron’s commentary with that of Woodside and other LNG exporters to see whether Australia becomes relatively less attractive within global LNG portfolios. title: Why Retirees Should Consider These 3 Ultra-Safe Dividend Stocks Now - The Globe and Mail
content: In a digitally connected world, Verizon Communications is a fixture in U.S. households. Altria Group's legacy business is on a slow burn, but it can still deliver hefty dividends. Chevron should still pay dividends despite global energy disruptions from the war in Iran. If you're retired and counting on dividend income to pay your bills, yield and reliability are at the top of your priority list. Those two things don't always go together. Oftentimes, high dividend yields signal potential problems with a stock. But there are steady companies in mature industries that have years, even decades, to their name, forging impressive track records not only of paying dividends but also increasing them. Will AI create the world's first trillionaire? Our team just released a report on the one little-known company, called an "Indispensable Monopoly" providing the critical technology Nvidia and Intel both need. Continue » Here are three blue chip dividend stocks with high yields, along with reasons retirees should consider them now. Three companies dominate the U.S. wireless network market, among them Verizon Communications(NYSE: VZ). Most Americans spend hours on their phones each day, making their phone bill a must-have in almost every household's monthly budget. It makes Verizon a resilient business that has increased its dividend for 20 consecutive years. Verizon stock currently yields 5.4%, and the dividend accounts for only 56% of its estimated earnings, a manageable payout ratio that provides an added financial buffer. Business should remain steady for Verizon in a world of increasing connectivity. The stock's forward price-to-earnings (P/E) ratio of just 10 times its 2026 estimated earnings makes Verizon stock a sound buy today. It seems hard to believe that Altria Group(NYSE: MO) is a Dividend King with over five decades of uninterrupted dividend increases. Smoking rates in the United States have declined for decades. Yet, the company, which sells Marlboro cigarettes and other tobacco and nicotine products, continues to thrive. Each year, it simply raises prices to offset declining cigarette volumes. Financially, Altria's dividend remains healthy, with a payout ratio of 75% of its estimated earnings. Raising prices should continue to work; analysts expect low-single-digit annualized earnings growth over the next three to five years. The stock yields a whopping 6.6% at its current price, and shares trade at 11 times 2026 earnings estimates, a reasonable price tag considering Altria's modest growth. The war in the Middle East has turned the energy industry upside-down. Despite the sudden uncertainty, retirees can still find safety in Chevron(NYSE: CVX). The integrated oil and gas giant has an extensive track record of navigating volatility, as evidenced by its 39 consecutive annual dividend increases. The stock has soared since the war in Iran began, but it still yields 3.4%. Chevron was already planning for at least 10% annualized free-cash-flow growth through 2030, but that forecast assumed Brent oil at $70 per barrel. With Brent hovering around $100, there could be considerably high upside to the dividend ahead. There's no telling how long the war might last, but it seems increasingly likely that oil prices could remain elevated. At the very least, retirees can count on Chevron's dividend moving forward. title: My 3 Highest Conviction Energy Stocks to Buy Amid All the Uncertainty Caused by the War With Iran - The Globe and Mail
content: Brookfield Renewable expects to grow its cash flow per share at a more than 10% annual rate over the next five years. Enbridge can deliver 5% annual growth through the end of the decade. Chevron can grow its free cash flow at a more than 10% annual rate at $70 oil. The war with Iran has upended the global energy markets. It has caused the biggest supply disruption in decades due to Iran's attacks on oil tankers exiting the Persian Gulf through the Strait of Hormuz, which handled 20% of global oil and liquified natural gas (LNG) volumes before the war. The longer the war rages, the bigger the global energy crisis could become. On a more positive note, the U.S. is seeking to end the conflict. If that happens, energy prices could fall sharply. Will AI create the world's first trillionaire? Our team just released a report on the one little-known company, called an "Indispensable Monopoly" providing the critical technology Nvidia and Intel both need. Continue » This uncertainty is making it much harder to invest in energy stocks. Here are my three highest-conviction energy stocks to buy despite all the uncertainty surrounding the war. Brookfield Renewable(NYSE: BEPC)(NYSE: BEP) is a leading global renewable energy producer. It operates hydroelectric, wind, solar, and battery storage assets secured by long-term power purchase agreements (PPAs) with utilities and large corporations. Most of its PPAs tie rates to inflation. As a result, Brookfield generates stable, steadily rising cash flow across market environments. Global renewable power demand is surging, driven by climate change concerns, energy security needs, and the rise of growth catalysts like AI data centers. Given the war's impact on global energy markets, it could drive increased investment in renewable energy in the future as countries seek greater energy security. Brookfield Renewable already expects to grow briskly in the coming years, targeting funds from operations per share growth of more than 10% annually through 2031. That should support continued dividend growth of 5% to 9% per year. With a 4% yield and double-digit earnings growth ahead, Brookfield should generate high-powered total returns in the coming years. Enbridge(NYSE: ENB) is one of North America's largest energy infrastructure companies. It transports 30% of the continent's crude oil, moves 20% of all gas consumed in the U.S., operates the largest gas utility franchise, and is a meaningful renewable power producer. These businesses generate extremely durable earnings (over 98% regulated or take-or-pay contracted). Enbridge's earnings are so predictable that it has achieved its annual financial guidance for 20 years in a row. That includes several turbulent periods in the energy market. The company has also increased its dividend (which yields over 5%) for 31 consecutive years (in Canadian dollars). Enbridge has a multi-billion-dollar backlog of commercially secured expansion projects that should come online through the early 2030s. The company expects its growth initiatives to fuel around 5% annual cash flow per share grow through the end of the decade. Add that to its high-yielding dividend, and Enbridge could deliver high-octane total returns in the coming years. Chevron(NYSE: CVX) has rebuilt its portfolio over the years to thrive at lower oil prices. It has sold off low-margin assets and invested heavily to acquire and develop low-cost, high-margin resources. As a result, Chevron has one of the lowest breakeven levels in the energy sector. It can fund its capital program and dividend at an average oil price below $50 a barrel through 2030. This year was already going to be a major inflection point for Chevron. It completed several large growth capital projects in 2025 and closed its needle-moving acquisition of Hess. As a result, the company expected to produce an additional $12.5 billion in free cash flow this year at $70 oil (from a base of $20.1 billion in 2025). Chevron is now on track to produce an even bigger free cash flow gusher this year. That will enable the company to repurchase shares at the high-end of its $10 billion to $20 billion target range while further strengthening its already elite balance sheet to weather future oil price declines. Chevron expects to grow its free cash flow at a more than 10% annual rate through 2030, assuming oil averages $70 a barrel. It could grow much faster if oil prices remain elevated. That should allow it to continue increasing its dividend, which it has done for 39 straight years. Brookfield Renewable, Enbridge, and Chevron can all thrive even if a Middle East peace deal pushes energy prices lower. That drives my high conviction in these energy stocks amid the current uncertainty. I expect them to create significant value for shareholders in the future, regardless of what happens in the energy market. title: Chevron Corp Stock (CVX) Moved Up by 3.23% on Mar 27: A Full Analysis - TradingKey
content: Chevron Corp (CVX) moved up by 3.23%. The Energy - Fossil Fuels sector is up by 1.91%. The company outperformed the industry. Top 3 stocks by turnover in the sector: Exxon Mobil Corp (XOM) up 3.00%; Chevron Corp (CVX) up 3.23%; Cheniere Energy Inc (LNG) up 4.68%. Chevron's stock exhibited upward movement today, primarily driven by persistent geopolitical tensions in the Middle East and concerns over global oil supply. The ongoing conflict, particularly the situation around the Strait of Hormuz, has kept crude oil prices elevated, boosting the energy sector. Chevron's CEO had previously highlighted the risk of markets underestimating potential oil supply disruptions should the Strait of Hormuz be impacted, a sentiment that contributes to investor apprehension and supports higher oil valuations. Macroeconomic factors continue to play a significant role, with oil prices remaining volatile but generally strong. Brent crude has been trading above $100 per barrel, and WTI above $90 per barrel, reflecting the tightening supply picture. The effective closure of the Strait of Hormuz for a significant portion of commercial traffic, which handles a large percentage of global oil, has led to a major shift in the global financial outlook and supply dynamics. OPEC+'s recent decision to gradually unwind some voluntary production adjustments starting in April 2026 suggests a recognition of healthy market fundamentals and low oil inventories, further reinforcing the perception of a tight supply environment. Company-specific strengths also contributed to the positive sentiment. Chevron recently announced new strategic deals in Venezuela and Equatorial Guinea, reinforcing its long-term strategy. The company reported strong performance in its latest quarter, including record worldwide production for 2025 and a four percent increase in its quarterly dividend. Analyst forecasts for 2026 have seen upward revisions for both revenue and earnings per share, with several firms upgrading their ratings and increasing price targets in recent weeks, indicating a constructive outlook for Chevron. However, the stock experienced intraday volatility, which can be attributed to a mix of factors. While oil prices have seen significant increases, there have been periods of fluctuation, including a recent slip amid hopes of a ceasefire in the Middle East, demonstrating the sensitivity of the market to geopolitical developments. Some analysts have recently downgraded Chevron to a "Hold" rating, suggesting that the stock may now be fairly valued after its recent appreciation, and noting that the current surge in oil prices is more supply-driven than demand-driven, which could present future headwinds. Additionally, some insider selling by executives in early March and a slight miss on revenue expectations in the latest reported quarter, along with potential legislative risks such as a proposed windfall profits tax, introduce elements of caution that can contribute to market fluctuations. Operational challenges, including disruptions from a tropical cyclone affecting Chevron's Australian operations, also add to market uncertainty. Technically, Chevron Corp (CVX) shows a MACD (12,26,9) value of [5.90], indicating a buy signal. The RSI at 77.64 suggests buy condition and the Williams %R at -8.28 suggests oversold condition. Please monitor closely. In terms of media coverage, Chevron Corp (CVX) shows a coverage score of 48, indicating a moderate level of media attention. The net profit is $12.30B, ranking 6 in the industry. title: Chevron (CVX) Valuation Check After Strong Multi‑Month Share Price Momentum - Sahm
content:
Chevron (CVX) Valuation Check After Strong Multi‑Month Share Price Momentum Chevron Corporation Chevron (CVX) has drawn fresh investor attention after a period of strong recent returns, with the stock up 1.3% over the past week, 12.1% over the past month, and 38.5% over the past 3 months. At a share price of $207.79, Chevron’s recent 30 day share price return of 12.11% and year to date gain of 33.28% point to strong momentum. The 1 year total shareholder return of 30.30% and 5 year total shareholder return of 141.97% show how recent moves sit within a much longer run of gains and shifting expectations around cash generation and risk. If Chevron’s momentum has you reviewing the energy space, it can be useful to see which other companies are drawing attention through the 26 power grid technology and infrastructure stocks With Chevron trading at $207.79, sitting close to its analyst price target yet carrying an indicated intrinsic discount of 44.39%, you have to ask: is there real value left here, or is the market already pricing in future growth? According to Vestra, Chevron’s fair value of $214.28 sits slightly above the recent close of $207.79. This setup results in a tight valuation gap built on detailed cash flow assumptions. The fair value for Chevron (CVX) is calculated using a Free Cash Flow Yield model and a 10% expected growth rate through 2030. Given the projected free cash flow expansion of $12.5 billion by 2026 and the record production levels, the fair value is estimated at $214.28 USD. Want to see what turns record production and higher free cash flow into that fair value? The narrative leans heavily on volume, margins, and disciplined capital spend assumptions, with one core cash flow lever doing most of the work. However, this hinges on oil prices staying supportive and the 15 to 20% workforce reduction landing cleanly; either could quickly challenge the cash flow story. That 3% undervaluation case sits awkwardly next to a very different signal. On earnings, Chevron trades on a P/E of 33.7x, compared with 16.6x for the US Oil and Gas industry and 26.9x for peers, while its fair ratio is 28.8x. That gap suggests investors are already paying up. Is this really a mispricing, or simply a rich cash flow story that is already priced in?
